unitTitle: Y9 Representations — going audiovisual (Teach Computing — adapted)

lessons.title: Binary mosaic — pixels, resolution and colour depth
lessons.objectives:
I can describe how a digital image is made of pixels (small squares)
I can explain that resolution means the number of pixels
I can explain that colour depth means the number of bits used for each pixel's colour
I can work out the number of bits in a small image
lessons.outline:
Routine first (same every lesson). Vocabulary on board: pixel · resolution · colour depth · bit · bitmap. TA: pixel-art editor (Scratch/Trinket) open for everyone; coloured grids ready for the unplugged build. Read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — from bits to pictures. Pupils recall that 0s and 1s are bits (starter worksheet, single choice) and LABEL pixel/row/column on a mosaic photo (drag, no writing).
   • Support: tick-box choice for “small square = pixel”.  • Core: write what “pixel” means.  • Challenge: why a neat grid is easy to store.
2. (5–35) Binary mosaic (unplugged + editor). Pairs build a small grid picture, give each pixel 1 bit (black/white), swap sequences and rebuild. Then move to the pixel-art editor.
   • Support: a printed grid to fill in.  • Core: build + describe.  • Challenge: use 2 bits per pixel (4 colours). Likely error: a pixel is NOT a colour — “a pixel is one square; its colour is stored as bits.” TA: prompt, do not do it for them.
3. (35–45) Terminology — pixels, resolution, colour depth, linked back to the mosaic (concept map slide; matching task on the activity worksheet).
4. (45–52) Count the bits (I do → we do → you do). Worked example 9×10 pixels × 3 bits = 270 bits; pairs do the calculation-practice table. Likely error: multiplying by the NUMBER OF COLOURS not the colour depth → “use bits per pixel.”
5. (52–55) Plenary — self-tick the 4 “I can…”; each pair shows their mosaic. TA notes who can count bits unaided.
Differentiation is on the SAME task, low floor / high ceiling — nobody’s screen looks “less done”. No flashing/sound on slides; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: A splash of colour — RGB and representation size
lessons.objectives:
I can describe how colour is made by mixing red, green and blue light
I can explain that RGB colour uses 8 bits for each colour, 24 bits in total
I can calculate the representation size of an image
I can describe the trade-off between file size and image quality
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: RGB · red/green/blue · colour depth · representation size · compression. TA: calculators out; colour-mixer site open. Read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — fill the gaps recap (starter worksheet): pixels · resolution · colour depth (fill-in-the-blank).
2. (5–20) Mixing light — RGB (I do → we do). Mixing red, green and blue LIGHT makes colours (lighter, unlike paint). Each colour set by an 8-bit number 0–255. Pairs try the colour mixer.
   • Support: choice — RGB = Red, Green, Blue; one pixel = 24 bits.  • Core: explain 8+8+8 = 24.  • Challenge: why “number of pixels” ≠ “number of colours”. Likely error: thinking RGB = 3 bits → “8 bits EACH for red, green and blue.”
3. (20–45) Representation size (you do). Worked example 800×600 × 24 bits = 11,520,000 bits; pairs complete the calculation-practice table (pixels, bits, convert to bytes). Likely error: multiplying by number of colours, or mixing bits and bytes → “÷8 to go bits → bytes.”
4. (45–52) Trade-off — size vs quality. More pixels / more colour depth = sharper but bigger; a fast website wants smaller.
5. (52–55) Plenary — self-tick the 4 “I can…”. TA notes who can do resolution × colour depth unaided.
Differentiation on the SAME task, low floor / high ceiling. No flashing/sound on slides; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Collage — image editing and ethics
lessons.objectives:
I can perform basic image edits using image-editing software
I can explain that editing an image is doing arithmetic on its binary numbers
I can describe one creative benefit of editing images
I can describe one ethical drawback of editing images
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: image editing · pixels · RGB · arithmetic on numbers · ethics. TA: GIMP installed and open; lesson image files saved locally. Read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — spot the edit (starter worksheet). One photo shown four ways; which is original? Single choice: editing = changing the picture’s numbers.
2. (5–10) Why editing works — a picture is numbers, so editing is arithmetic on those numbers (GIMP logo slide).
3. (10–45) Hands-on editing (you do). Everyone does Setup + Lighthouse, then chooses Owl / Ada / Penguin / Size-and-colours. Activity worksheet has the steps as an ORDER task.
   • Support: a printed step strip; the browser-simple tasks.  • Core: complete two tasks.  • Challenge: combine edits / Size-and-colours. Likely error: lost work → “Export/Save before you move on.” TA: if stuck, suggest a different task and return.
4. (45–53) Reflection — creative benefit vs ethical drawback (card-sort on the worksheet; think-write-pair-share). Is this image ‘real’?
5. (53–55) Plenary — self-tick the 4 “I can…”; show a finished edit. TA notes who can explain editing as arithmetic.
Differentiation on the SAME task; a calm role for anyone who prefers not to share their screen. No flashing/sound on slides; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Good vibrations — sound, sampling and bits
lessons.objectives:
I can recall that sound is a wave
I can explain what a microphone and a speaker each do
I can define the terms sample, sampling rate and sample size
I can describe how a sound is stored as a sequence of bits
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: sound · wave · microphone · speaker · analogue · digital · sample · sampling rate · sample size. TA: sound editor (Audacity) open; sound files saved locally; headphones available (optional). Read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–8) Starter — what is sound? Multi-select the words about sound (wave/vibration/loud, not pixel); play the pure tone and show the wave (starter worksheet).
2. (8–15) Sound is a wave — a vibrating object pushes the air; the push travels as a wave (waveform slide).
3. (15–22) Microphone and speaker — card-sort: microphone captures (sound→electricity), speaker plays (electricity→sound). Key idea: devices handle electricity, then numbers, not sound directly.
4. (22–40) Sampling (I do → we do). The computer measures the wave many times a second; each measurement is a sample. Matching task (sample / sampling rate / sample size ↔ meaning); ORDER task for the digitisation steps. Likely error: mixing sampling rate (per second) with sample size (bits per sample) → use the matching task.
5. (40–52) Explore a sound (you do) — open a sound, zoom to see the wave; paste a screenshot.
   • Support: a printed step strip.  • Core: describe what they see.  • Challenge: compare a tone vs speech wave.
6. (52–55) Plenary — self-tick the 4 “I can…”. TA notes who can define all three words.
Differentiation on the SAME task; a calm read-only role for anyone who prefers not to use headphones. No flashing/sound on slides beyond the one teacher-played tone.

lessons.title: Sonic playground — sound representation size
lessons.objectives:
I can calculate the representation size of a sound
I can explain how sampling rate affects size and quality
I can explain how sample size affects size and quality
I can perform basic sound edits using software
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: sampling rate · sample size · duration · channels · representation size · trade-off. TA: calculators out; Audacity open; sound files saved locally. Read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — fill the gaps recap (starter worksheet): sample · sampling rate · sample size (fill-in-the-blank).
2. (5–15) Build the size formula (I do → we do). Step by step: sampling rate × sample size × duration × channels (ORDER task on the worksheet). Worked example: 8000 samples/s × 1 byte × 1 s = 8000 bytes = 8 kB.
3. (15–25) Calculation practice (you do) — the sound-size table (bytes, convert to kB, a bits example, a stereo example). Likely error: forgetting channels, or mixing bits and bytes → “1 byte = 8 bits; ÷8 to convert.”
4. (25–35) Trade-offs — more samples/second or more bits/sample = better quality, bigger file (same idea as resolution and colour depth).
5. (35–52) Sound editing (you do) — open a sound in Audacity and try the editing tasks; paste a screenshot.
   • Support: a printed step strip.  • Core: complete a task.  • Challenge: the sound-editing challenges.
6. (52–55) Plenary — self-tick the 4 “I can…”. TA notes who can calculate a sound size unaided.
Differentiation on the SAME task, low floor / high ceiling. No flashing/sound on slides; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Always another way — other representations and compression
lessons.objectives:
I can recall that pixels and samples are not the only ways to store images and sound
I can describe vector images and MIDI as instructions, not pixels or samples
I can define compression
I can explain why compression is needed
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: vector graphics · MIDI · symbolic representation · compression · representation size. TA: the summative quiz ready (online tool if possible); vector emoji file + kingfisher files saved locally. Read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–25) Summative assessment — run 15–20 closed questions from the unit’s summative set, covering pixels/resolution/colour depth, image size, microphone/speaker, sampling, sound size and compression. Give whole-class feedback. (Starter worksheet has a quick recap to settle pupils first.)
2. (25–40) Another language — vector images store shapes/instructions; MIDI stores notes/instructions (not pixel-by-pixel or sample-by-sample). Teacher-played MIDI hook. Card-sort: pixels/samples vs instructions. Modify the vector emoji (browser or text-editor version).
   • Support: the browser version.  • Core: change a value and see it render.  • Challenge: the text-editor version.
3. (40–52) Compression — why files must be made smaller (storage, sending, processing). Numeric: kingfisher 2.2 MB → ~106 kB ≈ 20× smaller. Likely error: thinking the image lost pixels → “same resolution and colour depth; compression stores it more cleverly.”
4. (52–55) Plenary — confidence slider (1–5) + self-tick the 4 “I can…”. TA uses the slider to spot who needs another pass.
Differentiation on the SAME task, low floor / high ceiling. No flashing/sound on slides beyond the one teacher-played MIDI clip.
